project_and_lead_agency_information.lead_agency: City of Riverbend Community Development Department

project_location.within_2_miles_railways: Union Pacific mainline (east of site, approx. 1.2 miles)
project_location.within_2_miles_schools: Riverbend Elementary School; Northview Middle School; Sacramento Charter High School (within broader 2-mile radius)

local_action_type.other_local_action_type: Design Review approval; Tree Removal Permit; Grading Permit

development_type.educational: No stand‑alone educational facility is proposed. However, a small 2,500-square-foot community learning room within the mixed-use building will be available for after-school tutoring, adult ESL classes, and neighborhood meetings in coordination with Riverbend Elementary School and the local community college.
development_type.recreational: Approximately 6.8 acres of public and private recreational amenities, including a 3.2-acre central neighborhood park with a multi-use turf field, children’s playground, picnic shelters, a half basketball court, and a small splash pad; 2.1 acres of linear greenway and multi-use trails along the project’s western boundary; and 1.5 acres of private recreational courtyards, a community garden, and a small dog park distributed within the multifamily residential clusters.
development_type.transportation_type: On-site public streets, roundabouts, bicycle lanes, sidewalks, and transit amenities, including a new signalized intersection at Riverbend Road and a pair of bus pull‑outs and shelters to accommodate Sacramento Regional Transit local bus service.
development_type.other_development_type: Public infrastructure improvements including underground utilities, a stormwater detention basin with integrated bioretention features, and streetscape landscaping along Garden Highway and Riverbend Road.

project_issues_discussed_in_document.other_project_issues_discussed_in_docume: Energy consumption and greenhouse gas emissions; environmental justice and community health; construction vibration impacts to nearby residences; shade and urban heat island reduction strategies; and climate resilience, including design responses to potential future changes in Sacramento River hydrology.

land_use_and_project_description.present_land_use_zoning_general_plan_des: The approximately 48.6-acre project site is currently designated as “Suburban Neighborhood Low Density Residential” in the City of Riverbend General Plan, with an allowed density range of 3–8 dwelling units per acre. Zoning is primarily R-1 (Single-Family Residential) with a small portion along Garden Highway zoned C-2 (General Commercial). Existing on-site uses include two single-family residences with associated outbuildings, fallow pasture historically used for horse grazing, several scattered ornamental and riparian trees (including valley oak, cottonwood, and Chinese pistache), and an informal dirt access road running north–south. Surrounding uses include single-family residential neighborhoods to the east, agricultural and open space lands to the south, the Sacramento River corridor to the west, and a mix of commercial and light industrial uses to the north near the I‑5 interchange.
land_use_and_project_description.project_description:
The Riverbend Mixed-Use Village Project is a comprehensive infill development that seeks to transform an underutilized, largely fallow 48.6-acre site at the edge of the City of Riverbend into a compact, walkable, and transit-supportive neighborhood that includes a mix of housing types, neighborhood-serving commercial and office space, and publicly accessible open space amenities.

At a high level, the project would develop:
- Up to 312 residential units in a mix of townhomes, small-lot single-family homes, and three- to four-story multifamily buildings with ground-floor commercial along the primary internal main street.
- Approximately 68,250 square feet of neighborhood-serving commercial space oriented along Garden Highway and the new main street, including a small-format grocery store (approximately 25,000 square feet), café and restaurant spaces, a pharmacy, and various local retail and service tenants.
- Approximately 48,000 square feet of two- to three-story professional office space intended for small medical offices, professional services, and co-working space suitable for local entrepreneurs and remote workers.
- Roughly 6.8 acres of parks, trails, and other recreational amenities, including a central neighborhood park, greenways, and a small dog park.
- Supporting infrastructure such as new public streets, utilities, stormwater management facilities, and pedestrian and bicycle connections to surrounding neighborhoods.

Residential Component:
The residential program is designed to provide a range of housing options for different household types and income levels. Approximately 96 units would be developed as attached townhomes and small-lot single-family units fronting on local streets with rear-loaded garages accessed via alleys. These units would generally be two to three stories in height and are targeted to first-time homebuyers and move-down households. The remaining 216 units would be located in three- to four-story multifamily buildings organized around internal courtyards, with a mix of studios, one-bedroom, two-bedroom, and three-bedroom apartments. A minimum of 10 percent of the total units (approximately 31 units) are anticipated to be reserved as affordable to low- and moderate-income households, subject to a separate affordable housing agreement with the City.

Commercial and Office Uses:
The commercial and office components are intended to serve both project residents and the surrounding community, reducing vehicle miles traveled by providing daily needs within walking and biking distance. The largest commercial space would be a small-format neighborhood grocery store at the corner of Garden Highway and the new main street, with smaller retail bays along the main street that could accommodate uses such as a coffee shop, bakery, neighborhood pub, hair salon, dry cleaner, fitness studio, and other local-serving businesses. The office buildings will be located primarily along the northern portion of the site, with parking lots screened by landscaping and building orientation designed to provide an active street frontage while buffering nearby residential areas from freeway noise.

Open Space and Recreation:
The project includes a 3.2-acre central neighborhood park that will be dedicated to the City, featuring a flexible turf field for youth sports and informal recreation, a children’s play area with age-separated equipment, a small splash pad, picnic tables and barbecue areas, and a shade structure that can be used for community events such as neighborhood movie nights or farmers’ markets. A multi-use trail will run along the western edge of the site, connecting to an existing informal riverfront path and providing opportunities for walking, jogging, and bicycling. Internal pedestrian paseos and greenways will connect residential clusters to the park, commercial areas, and transit stops, with wayfinding signage, benches, and pedestrian-scale lighting.

Circulation and Access:
Primary vehicular access will be provided via a new signalized intersection on Riverbend Road, roughly opposite an existing collector street serving the adjacent neighborhood. A secondary right-in/right-out driveway will be located on Garden Highway to serve the commercial and office uses. The internal circulation system will consist of a modified grid of local streets with on-street parking and traffic-calming features such as curb extensions, raised crosswalks, and a small roundabout at the central park. Bicycle facilities will include striped bike lanes on the main internal street and shared-use paths connecting to off-site bike routes. The project will construct two new bus pull-outs and shelters along Riverbend Road in coordination with Sacramento Regional Transit, with the goal of providing direct transit access to downtown Riverbend and the Sacramento International Airport.

Utilities and Infrastructure:
All existing on-site septic systems and private wells will be abandoned in accordance with applicable regulations. The project will connect to the City’s municipal water system via an extension of an existing 12-inch water main in Riverbend Road, with on-site distribution lines sized to provide adequate domestic and fire flow. Sanitary sewer service will be provided by connecting to an existing trunk line located east of the site, with on-site gravity mains and manholes designed to meet capacity and maintenance requirements. Stormwater runoff will be managed through a combination of low-impact development (LID) features such as bioretention planters, permeable paving in selected parking areas, and a centrally located detention basin at the southern end of the site that will attenuate peak flows and provide water quality treatment prior to discharge to the existing drainage system leading to Arcade Creek. All dry utilities (electric, gas, telecommunications) will be placed underground.

Design Features and Sustainability:
The project will incorporate a variety of design features intended to promote sustainability and climate resilience. Buildings will be oriented to maximize natural light and cross-ventilation, with shading devices and street trees helping to reduce the urban heat island effect. Rooftop solar photovoltaic systems are anticipated on the multifamily and commercial structures, and EV-ready infrastructure will be installed in a portion of residential garages and common parking areas. The landscape palette will emphasize drought-tolerant, native, and regionally appropriate plant species, with efficient irrigation systems and soil amendments to improve water retention. The project will comply with current California Green Building Standards Code (CALGreen) requirements and may pursue additional green building certifications for certain buildings if feasible.

Phasing:
Development is expected to occur in three major phases over approximately 6–8 years, subject to market conditions. Phase 1 would likely include initial backbone infrastructure, the central park, and a portion of the residential units. Phase 2 would complete the majority of residential buildout and initiate the commercial main street. Phase 3 would focus on the remaining commercial and office development and final infrastructure connections. Construction activities will generally occur during daytime hours on weekdays, with best management practices implemented to control dust, noise, and stormwater runoff.

Overall, the Riverbend Mixed-Use Village Project is intended to create a cohesive, pedestrian-friendly neighborhood that supports local housing needs, provides convenient access to daily goods and services, and enhances public access to recreational amenities, while carefully managing environmental impacts through thoughtful site design, mitigation measures, and compliance with applicable local, state, and federal regulations.
